blocks.description: For 100 orders, this executes 101 database queries -- one for the order list and one for each order's customer. This is the classic N+1 problem.
A single JOIN query replaces 101 queries with 1. Always think about how many queries your endpoint actually executes.
blocks.code:
@app.route("/api/orders")
def get_orders():
    # GOOD: one query gets everything with a JOIN
    rows = db.execute("""
        SELECT orders.id, orders.total, customers.name AS customer_name
        FROM orders
        JOIN customers ON customers.id = orders.customer_id
        ORDER BY orders.created_at DESC
        LIMIT 100
    """).fetchall()

    return jsonify([
        {
            "id": row["id"],
            "total": row["total"],
            "customer_name": row["customer_name"],
        }
        for row in rows
    ])  # 1 query for 100 orders!
